messages.content: You are a SQL developer. Write correct, efficient SQL queries.
SELECT department, COUNT(DISTINCT salary) AS distinct_salary_count, COUNT(employee_id) AS total_employees FROM payroll GROUP BY department;

SELECT customers.name AS customer_name, transactions.amount AS transaction_amount, transactions.date AS transaction_date FROM customers INNER JOIN transactions ON customers.customer_id = transactions.customer_id;

SELECT origin || '-' || destination AS route, MAX(daily_total) AS max_daily_passengers FROM (SELECT origin, destination, departure_date, SUM(passengers) AS daily_total FROM flights GROUP BY origin, destination, departure_date) GROUP BY origin, destination;

SELECT customer, product, price * quantity AS total_spent FROM orders WHERE quantity > 2;

SELECT m.name AS member_name, p.plan_name AS plan_name, p.monthly_fee AS monthly_fee FROM members m JOIN memberships ms ON m.member_id = ms.member_id JOIN plans p ON ms.plan_id = p.plan_id ORDER BY m.name;

SELECT title,
       CASE
           WHEN pages <= 200 THEN 'short'
           WHEN pages <= 350 THEN 'medium'
           ELSE 'long'
       END AS category,
       CASE
           WHEN pages <= 200 THEN 'easy'
           WHEN pages <= 350 THEN 'moderate'
           ELSE 'challenging'
       END AS reading_level
FROM books;
You are a SQL developer. Write correct, efficient SQL queries.

WITH stream_counts AS (
    SELECT song_id, COUNT(*) AS stream_count, SUM(duration_seconds) AS total_duration
    FROM streams
    GROUP BY song_id
)
SELECT song_id, stream_count AS total_streams, total_duration
FROM stream_counts
WHERE stream_count > 1;
You are a SQL developer. Write correct, efficient SQL queries.
SELECT t.name AS team_name, COALESCE(SUM(m.home_score), 0) AS total_home_goals FROM teams t LEFT JOIN matches m ON t.id = m.home_team_id GROUP BY t.id, t.name ORDER BY team_name;
You are a SQL developer. Write correct, efficient SQL queries.
SELECT dish, (price * quantity) AS total_revenue FROM restaurant_orders ORDER BY total_revenue DESC LIMIT 3;
You are a SQL developer. Write correct, efficient SQL queries.
SELECT p.user_id AS user_id, p.post_id AS post_id, p.likes AS likes FROM posts p WHERE p.likes > (SELECT AVG(likes) FROM posts WHERE user_id = p.user_id);

Problem: List the customer_name and total_amount of the top 3 highest total_amount orders.
Language: sql
Schema:
CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_name TEXT, total_amount REAL, order_date TEXT); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (1, 'Alice', 25.50, '2024-01-15'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (2, 'Bob', 42.00, '2024-01-16'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (3, 'Charlie', 18.75, '2024-01-17'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (4, 'Diana', 63.20, '2024-01-18'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (5, 'Eve', 31.00, '2024-01-19'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (6, 'Frank', 55.40, '2024-01-20');
Write a single correct SQL query.
SELECT customer_name, total_amount FROM orders ORDER BY total_amount DESC LIMIT 3
You are a SQL developer. Write correct, efficient SQL queries.

WITH user_stats AS (
    SELECT
        user_id,
        COUNT(*) AS stream_count,
        SUM(duration_seconds) AS total_duration,
        AVG(duration_seconds) AS avg_duration
    FROM streams
    GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
    user_id AS user_id,
    stream_count AS total_streams,
    total_duration AS total_duration
FROM user_stats
WHERE avg_duration > 200;
You are a SQL developer. Write correct, efficient SQL queries.

        user_id,

    FROM listens

)

    user_id AS user_id,
    total_listens AS total_listens,
    unique_songs AS unique_songs,
    avg_duration AS avg_duration
FROM user_stats;

LEFT JOIN matches away ON t.id = away.away_team_id

WITH frequent_users AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM streams
    WHERE song_id = 101
    GROUP BY user_id
    HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2
)
SELECT s.user_id AS user_id,
       101 AS song_id,
       SUM(s.duration_seconds) AS total_listen_seconds
FROM streams s
JOIN frequent_users fu ON s.user_id = fu.user_id
WHERE s.song_id = 101
GROUP BY s.user_id;

WITH user_totals AS (
    SELECT user_id, SUM(duration_seconds) AS total_seconds
    FROM streams
    GROUP BY user_id
),
average AS (
    SELECT AVG(total_seconds) AS avg_seconds
    FROM user_totals
)
SELECT u.user_id AS user_id,
       u.total_seconds AS total_seconds,
       CASE WHEN u.total_seconds > a.avg_seconds THEN 'yes' ELSE 'no' END AS above_average
FROM user_totals u
CROSS JOIN average a;

WITH user_stats AS (
    SELECT user_id,
           SUM(duration_seconds) AS total_duration,
           COUNT(DISTINCT track_id) AS track_count
    FROM streams
    GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT user_id,
       total_duration,
       track_count
FROM user_stats
WHERE total_duration > 400;

WITH song_count AS (
    SELECT user_id, COUNT(DISTINCT song_id) AS distinct_songs
    FROM streams
    GROUP BY user_id
    HAVING distinct_songs >= 2
)
SELECT s.user_id AS user_id,
       COUNT(*) AS total_streams,
       SUM(s.duration_seconds) AS total_duration
FROM streams s
JOIN song_count sc ON s.user_id = sc.user_id
GROUP BY s.user_id;
